Hi Greg. I am just waiting for my coffee. This place is always so busy in the morning.
It really is! I come here every day. Honestly, I love their coffee.
So do I. It is the best in the neighborhood.
Are they open on Sundays? I can never remember.
I think so, but I am not completely sure.
By the way, I do not really enjoy the long line at the counter.
Neither do I. It moves so slowly on busy mornings.
I want a small snack too. Which one do you recommend?
The lemon one is great. Anyway, it was nice to chat. Have a good day!
So do I. I feel exactly the same.
Neither do I. I do not enjoy that either.
I think so, but I am not sure.
I would like the small one, please.
To sum up, we agreed on almost everything.

People think good conversation is about having a lot to say, but honestly, it is often the opposite. The best talkers I know say less, not more. When I agree with someone, I do not repeat their whole sentence. If a friend says he loves a restaurant, I just say, So do I. If someone asks whether the shop is open, I say, I think so, and that is enough. When I do not like something and my friend does not either, I say, Neither do I, and we both smile. The little words carry the meaning: so, one, do, neither. You leave out the obvious part, and the conversation moves faster and feels warmer. The funny thing is, saying less actually makes people feel more connected to you, not less.

Hi Ana Paula, it is Sarah! I just wanted to tell you about the nicest little chat I had this morning. I ran into my new neighbor at the mailbox, and we made small talk for about five minutes. It was so easy and casual. When I said I loved the quiet street, she said, So do I, and when I said I did not enjoy the traffic downtown, she said, Neither do I. We agreed on everything without saying much at all! She asked if the corner cafe was any good, and I just said, I think so. To sum up, it was a two-minute conversation, but I walked away feeling like I had made a friend. Anyway, call me back when you can. Talk soon, bye!

I met my neighbor Rita for a brief chat at the bakery. At first we just made small talk about the weekend. When she said she loved the coffee, I said, So do I. She asked if the shop was open on Sunday, and I said, I think so. To sum up, the whole conversation felt easy and natural.
